title: My negative experience with higher education

transcript.text: Hello. This video is entitled, My Negative Experience with Higher Education.
I decided to film this video just to share my story, share what I think needs to change,
And also to ask people who watch this video, at least one person, if one person could just type in your opinion about what you think I'm saying.
Because it's kind of an interesting story to me.
And the story goes like this, I failed out of the education department in my college, so I'm a failure.
They failed me.
I went to college, Fort Lewis College in Durango, Colorado, which, by the way, is not, it's a total kind of a party college.
It's, you know, I knew kids who didn't even graduate from junior high, dropped out of school after seventh grade and got into that school.
And, you know, I had a friend who didn't even graduate high school and got into that school.
And, you know, I went to the school, and, like, I never had to do homework for my classes.
I just parted the whole time.
And, you know, now, but at the same time, I majored in English education because I wanted to be, I majored in English because I liked writing, and I thought I was going to be able to write.
And I never was, actually.
It was mainly just a kind of, like, history class for English, kind of like the Industrial Revolution in England and English writers in the 1800s.
And, you know, that was kind of interesting.
I got to learn about what England was like in the Industrial Revolution.
It was a pretty horrible place, man.
You'd be glad you live now and not then.
But, anyways, and education because I wanted to be a teacher because I like learning stuff and explaining it to people.
But it didn't work out that way.
And this is what happened.
And I guess it all started when I was, like, a freshman or something or a sophomore.
I think I was a sophomore or a junior.
No, no, I think I was a junior, actually.
I can't remember.
I was, like, 20 or 21 or something.
Um, and I, um, I, uh, I was doing my practicum hours.
See, I, in my, uh, in the school I did, I took all the education classes.
I did 100 practicum hours.
Um, I did everything.
All I needed to do was do, uh, four months of, uh, student teaching and then I would have had my English, uh, my, uh, Colorado teaching license.
But, like, I guess, uh, one of those, one of the first practicum hours, I was, I went to the high school in Durango.
And I was, uh, I guess they put me into the wacky kids class.
I guess the alternative kids class.
And I was, uh, supposed to, I guess, tutor this girl.
It's, like, a really cool girl.
Like, super kayaker girl.
Been down the Grand Canyon twice and the Cataract Canyon twice on a kayak.
My name is Claire.
And she's all tripping on acid.
And I'm all, like, hanging out with her.
And then I'm done and I'm, like, I didn't want to go back in the high school because I didn't want to, like, get lost and not know where the bathroom was.
So I kind of stuck behind a shed and I peed.
And I guess I didn't notice that their security guard guy saw me.
He runs up and he's, like, what you doing?
I'm, like, oh, yeah, what's up?
I'm, like, yeah, I'm just here doing the student teaching.
He's, like, what's your name?
I'm, like, Kyle Pounds.
I'm, like, all right, cool.
Anyway, next, next day of class, the, like, the head of the teacher program or whatever pulls me out of class.
And he's, like, we're going to have to ask you to drop out of the whole teacher education program.
And I go, what?
Why?
And he goes, because you were busted peeing on the high school lawn.
And I was, like, what?
Well, I'm not going to drop out for that.
And he goes, well, you're going to have to talk to our main boss guy.
So I go and talk to the main boss guy.
I don't think I'll name any names because I talk to the main boss guy.

And he's all, yeah, dude, going to have to ask you to drop out because you're not, because I just, I just had to, I just got talking, like, a half hour having to explain myself because the principal of the high school was just blasting me a new, a new ear.
You're telling me how I could possibly allow a scumbag like you to enter the, you know, our esteemed education program in our school.
And it's, like, in this school?
You know, and it was weird because it's, like, at that time they needed 600,000 new teachers in America.
Teachers only get, like, $30,000 a year.
It's not, like, the most coveted job in the world.
Teachers work from 7 in the morning to, like, late at night every day and have to volunteer and work on weekends and all this stuff.
It's not, you know.
I was like, what?
I was like, no way, dude, I'm not going to.
He was like, plus you have to cut those dreadlocks off your head.
And I'm like, what are you talking about?
I'm in school.
I'm paying you guys.
You work for me.
He was like, no, if you're going to do student, if you're going to do, go into these schools and do student teaching or whatever, kids will get distracted.
And I'm like, what are you talking about?
He's like, hey, man, you don't understand.
When I was your age, I got arrested just for walking across the street because I had long hair.
And I go, well, that was the 60s, and I'm not even doing student teaching.
I'm the student now.
I'm 20 years old.
And I'm like, no, dude, I'm not going to drop out of your thing.
I'm going to be a teacher.
And they're like, ah, well, we can't make you.
So anyways, so anyway, and then a couple years later, I'm about to graduate.
And now it was pretty clear that these guys didn't like me because one of them was all like, you know, are you still living out of your van?
And I'm like, yeah.
And then another guy goes, you know, I have bad handwriting.
And then they failed me in my, in my, in my, like, presentation test.
Are you supposed to prove that you know how to talk in front of people?
And so then I had to take it again, and I guess they passed me there.

But anyways, I, I had the one final class called Methods of Teaching, and where you're supposed to make a three minute long, I mean, I think like a two or a three minute long, five minute long presentation of a three week long lesson plan of something that you make.
And I made one that was 50 pages long.
I kind of went overboard on this, kind of collecting all these other pages I do.
Basically, most of those pages were like example papers, because I did one for the, the paper I did, I thought was really good.
It was, um, it was, um, a three week, it was a, uh, a research paper that you do where you, where all the kids, uh, first do note cards.
And then they all, well, they all share like what they want to do the ideas about, and then they share the, um, their notes with the rest of the school.
It's kind of like progressive, and they all, uh, interactive with all the students together.
And, um, and, uh, anyway, they're supposed to follow the syllabus.
I can't believe I, I can't remember the, uh, my paper.
But, um, it's, um, it was, uh, anyway, it was, it was, it was for a, um, a research paper that you write.
And, uh, anyway, I thought it was really good.
And, uh, I had a class of about ten, and ten kids, ten people.
And, uh, the teacher had all the ten people do their presentation, and all the other guys were really, really simple stuff.
Um, just like games.
One guy just had a game, and he was done in a minute.
And then it was my turn, but it was the very last day, and they only gave me one minute.
Uh, and I, yeah, yeah, I think, I think your presentation was supposed to last like ten or twenty minutes.
So, but I got one minute into my first presentation, and the bell rang, and that was the very last day of the class.
So I actually never got to give it at all.
I only got to do, like, a quick introduction for about a minute, and then it was over.
And, um, so then I, uh, the teacher, it was like, became grade time.
And the teacher goes, oh, I can't give you a grade because you have to fix your sample, um, papers.
Oh, yeah, I remember what my paper was about.
It was about, yeah, you make the, uh, the, uh, rough draft, and then you make the, um, the, uh, you fix the draft, and then you, then you have a paper.
Anyway, all of my rough drafts and stuff, he didn't like them.
He thought they were too politically charged.
So I'm like, all right, cool.
So I fixed those, and then he made me fix, um, do something else.
Yeah, I remember what it was, and then I fixed that.
And then he goes, oh, yeah, the title of one of your papers has to be a title of, um, has to switch over the title of this.
Like, switch the titles on two of the chapters.
And then he wanted me to do something else that was just as, like, trivial.
And I didn't understand, I didn't, like, agree with him, so I just didn't do it.
And then, like, the next year I came back because I had to finish the semester.
And he goes, oh, yeah, I gave you a grade, but it wasn't very good.
And I was like, all right, whatever.
And then they had me for the student teaching.
And I met the woman, and all of a sudden something happened with the woman, and she couldn't do the student teaching with me.
So then I was, so then I was like, all right, well, I got to go.
And I was traveling around the world for two years.
I came home, and then I was like, I called him up, and I go, oh, yeah, I'm going to do my student teaching.
And they go, you can't do the student teaching because you got a D minus in that class.
So you're screwed.
You're not going to get your teaching license.
Because I got a D minus in that class.
But they never told me.
He never told me I had a D minus.
He said my grade wasn't very good.
So, like, is that fair?
Was I gypped?
So anyways, I'm out of time.
